content.text: Scientific discovery pipelines are automated, end-to-end workflows that chain data ingestion, preprocessing, feature engineering, model execution, validation, and reporting into a reproducible computational pipeline orchestrated by an LLM agent. They go beyond single-agent discovery by composing multiple specialised components into a durable, versioned workflow that can be triggered, monitored, and re-run as new data arrives or as pipeline stages need revision.

attrs.alt: Scientific Discovery Pipeline: data ingestion → preprocessing → feature engineering → model/analysis → validation → LLM orchestrator → feedback loop + results store + discovery report

content.text: A scientific discovery pipeline is a structured, automated workflow that takes raw scientific data as input and produces discovery outputs (validated findings, hypotheses, paper drafts) through a series of well-defined, versioned computational stages. Each stage is a separate component with defined inputs, outputs, and quality metrics. The pipeline is orchestrated by an LLM agent that decides which stages to run, how to interpret intermediate results, and when to iterate vs. proceed.

The distinction from a single discovery agent is modularity and durability. A pipeline persists its state across runs, so a multi-day experimental workflow can be interrupted and resumed without losing progress. Stages can be independently versioned and swapped (e.g., replacing a random forest model with a gradient boosting model in the analysis stage without changing the preprocessing or reporting stages). This modular architecture enables scientific software engineering practices: unit testing individual stages, A/B testing pipeline configurations, and reproducible experiment tracking.

Scientific discovery pipelines are commonly implemented using workflow orchestration tools (Snakemake, Nextflow, Prefect, Airflow) for the computational stages, with an LLM orchestrator layer that provides natural-language planning and interpretation capabilities on top of the deterministic pipeline execution. The LLM does not run the computational tools directly — it calls them through well-defined APIs or tool-use schemas and interprets their outputs.

Reproducibility is the central crisis in computational science. A survey of Nature papers found that 70% of researchers could not reproduce other scientists' results, and 50% could not even reproduce their own. Scientific discovery pipelines address this directly by encoding the full experimental workflow as code: every data transformation, model hyperparameter, and analysis step is explicitly versioned and recorded. Running the same pipeline on the same data always produces the same output.

Scientific pipelines also enable scaling. A pipeline that works on one dataset can be applied to 1,000 datasets through batch execution. A genomics analysis pipeline that takes 3 days to run manually can be applied to an entire cohort in parallel on a cloud cluster with minimal incremental effort. The LLM orchestrator adds the intelligence to adapt the pipeline configuration per dataset (e.g., adjusting quality thresholds based on data characteristics) rather than applying a rigid one-size-fits-all configuration.
Reproducibility: pipeline as code ensures every run is traceable to exact inputs, parameters, and software versions.
Scalability: run the same pipeline on hundreds of datasets in parallel without incremental workflow design.
Modularity: swap out individual pipeline stages without disrupting others.
Continuous execution: trigger pipelines automatically as new data arrives.
Failure recovery: resume from the last successful stage after a failure rather than re-running from scratch.
Collaboration: version-controlled pipeline code can be shared, reviewed, and extended across research teams.
LLM orchestration adds flexibility: natural-language interpretation of intermediate results and adaptive re-planning.
Engineering overhead: building a proper pipeline requires more upfront engineering than running ad hoc scripts.
Rigidity risk: over-specified pipelines can be hard to adapt to unexpected data characteristics.
Orchestration complexity: combining a workflow scheduler with an LLM orchestrator adds architectural complexity.
LLM non-determinism: the LLM's planning decisions may differ between runs, introducing variability in an otherwise deterministic pipeline.
Tool integration cost: connecting computational tools (BLAST, RDKit, scipy) to the LLM orchestrator requires writing tool schemas and wrappers.

A scientific discovery pipeline is defined as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of stages. Each stage has: a unique name, input file patterns or data specifications, an executable command or Python function, output specifications, resource requirements (CPU, GPU, memory), and quality checks that must pass before downstream stages execute. The DAG structure encodes dependencies: stage B runs only after stage A completes successfully.

The LLM orchestrator runs at two levels. At the planning level, it receives the research objective and the available pipeline components and selects which stages to include, in what order, with what parameters. At the interpretation level, it receives intermediate results after key stages and decides whether to proceed, revise parameters and re-run the stage, or branch to an alternative analysis path. This interpretation loop is what transforms a static pipeline into an adaptive discovery system.

Version control of pipeline configurations (stage parameters, model hyperparameters, tool versions) is implemented using a configuration management system (DVC, MLflow, or a custom YAML-based registry). Every pipeline run records its exact configuration, enabling exact reproduction of any past result and comparison of results across configuration variants.

The diagram above shows the pipeline architecture. Raw data enters through the Data Ingestion stage and flows through Preprocessing, Feature Engineering, Model/Analysis, and Validation stages. The LLM Orchestrator sits above the pipeline, planning stage execution, interpreting results, and triggering re-runs or revisions. Outputs flow to three destinations: the Feedback Loop (which can trigger upstream stages to re-run with revised parameters), the Results Store (which persists findings with full provenance), and the Discovery Report generator.
When to Use Scientific Discovery Pipelines
Multi-stage computational experiments that require deterministic reproducibility.
Workflows applied repeatedly to new data as it arrives.
Collaborative research projects where multiple teams contribute to different pipeline stages.
High-throughput screening workflows (genomics, drug discovery, materials science).
Experiments requiring resource management (GPU-intensive ML stages mixed with CPU-intensive analysis).
Example 2 — Snakemake Pipeline with LLM Interpretation

# Snakemake pipeline (snakefile) for genomics discovery
# The LLM orchestrator runs after key stages to interpret results

# snakefile content (saved as Snakefile in project dir)
SNAKEFILE_CONTENT = """
rule all:
    input: "results/pathway_report.txt"

rule quality_control:
    input: "data/raw_counts.tsv"
    output: "results/qc_report.html"
    shell: "multiqc {input} -o results/qc"

rule normalise:
    input: "data/raw_counts.tsv"
    output: "results/normalised_counts.tsv"
    script: "scripts/normalise.py"

rule differential_expression:
    input:
        counts="results/normalised_counts.tsv",
        metadata="data/sample_metadata.tsv"
    output: "results/de_results.tsv"
    script: "scripts/run_deseq2.R"

rule enrichment_analysis:
    input: "results/de_results.tsv"
    output: "results/enrichment_results.tsv"
    script: "scripts/gsea.py"

rule pathway_report:
    input: "results/enrichment_results.tsv"
    output: "results/pathway_report.txt"
    script: "scripts/generate_report.py"
"""

import anthropic
import subprocess
import json

def run_pipeline_with_llm(snakefile_path: str, objective: str) -> str:
    """Run Snakemake pipeline and use LLM to interpret results."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    # Run up to the DE analysis stage
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["snakemake", "--until", "differential_expression", "-j4"],
        capture_output=True, text=True, cwd="/path/to/project"
    )

    if result.returncode != 0:
        return f"Pipeline failed: {result.stderr[:500]}"

    # Read DE results
    try:
        with open("results/de_results.tsv") as f:
            de_preview = f.read()[:2000]
    except FileNotFoundError:
        de_preview = "Results not found"

    # LLM interprets DE results
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Interpret these differential expression results for: {objective}

DE results preview:
{de_preview}

Summarise:
1. Top upregulated and downregulated genes
2. Whether the pattern suggests biological processes to investigate
3. Quality flags (e.g., too many or too few DE genes)
4. Recommended next analysis steps
"""}]
    )

    interpretation = response.content[0].text

    # Continue pipeline with full analysis
    subprocess.run(["snakemake", "-j4"], cwd="/path/to/project")

    return interpretation

result = run_pipeline_with_llm("Snakefile", "Identify gene expression changes in cancer vs normal tissue")
print(result[:1000])

The Broad Institute's Terra platform combines Snakemake/WDL pipeline execution with cloud storage and compute scheduling for large-scale genomics discovery. A pipeline processing a cohort of 10,000 genomes for variant discovery and association analysis runs across hundreds of cloud VMs in parallel, with each stage checkpointed to ensure failure recovery. LLM-based interpretation was added as a layer that receives quality control reports from each pipeline stage and generates natural-language summaries flagging anomalies — samples with coverage below threshold, unexpected batch effects, or unexpectedly high variant counts — for bioinformatician review.

In drug discovery, Schrödinger's FEP+ pipeline for free energy perturbation calculations represents a similar architecture: a computational chemistry pipeline with defined stages (system preparation, MD equilibration, FEP production runs, analysis) orchestrated by an automated scheduler. The addition of LLM interpretation allows the system to read docking score distributions and flag compounds that appear to be outliers — unexpectedly high or low scores that warrant closer inspection — before the expensive FEP calculation stage.
No stage-level checkpointing: without checkpoints, a failure in the last stage requires re-running the entire pipeline.
Hardcoded file paths: use relative paths and configuration files; hardcoded paths break when running on different machines.
Missing resource specifications: not specifying CPU/memory/GPU requirements leads to stage contention and pipeline failures in cluster environments.
No input validation: accepting malformed data at the ingestion stage propagates errors through all downstream stages.
LLM interpretation not logged: the LLM's interpretation of results is a pipeline output that must be versioned alongside computational outputs.
Use a workflow manager (Snakemake, Nextflow, Prefect) rather than shell scripts for reproducible, checkpointed execution.
Define all pipeline parameters in a configuration file (YAML/JSON) versioned alongside the pipeline code.
Implement stage-level quality checks that block downstream execution when QC thresholds are not met.
Log all LLM interpretation outputs alongside computational results for full reproducibility.
Use content-addressed storage (DVC, IPFS) for large data files so pipeline provenance is independent of file location.
Test each pipeline stage independently with unit tests before integrating into the full pipeline.
Python-based; Makefile-style DAGs; excellent HPC support
Groovy DSL; excellent for bioinformatics; Docker/Singularity
Git for large files; pipeline stage caching
Parameters, metrics, artefact logging per run
Cloud-native; Python-first; easy retries and caching

Scientific discovery pipelines often process sensitive data (patient genomics, proprietary compound libraries). Implement strict input validation and output sanitisation at each stage boundary. Access controls must restrict which users and services can read or modify pipeline inputs, outputs, and configuration. In regulated research environments, pipeline execution logs must be tamper-proof and retained for the required period.
Hash all input files at ingestion and verify at each stage to detect data corruption or tampering.
Run pipeline stages in isolated containers (Docker) to prevent dependency conflicts and ensure portability.
Implement secrets management for API keys (database credentials, cloud storage tokens) using environment variables or secret managers, never hardcoded.
Require code review for all pipeline configuration changes in regulated environments.
Back up the results store with automated snapshots; scientific datasets are irreplaceable.
Scientific discovery pipelines combine deterministic workflow execution with LLM-based interpretation to create adaptive, reproducible research workflows.
Workflow managers (Snakemake, Nextflow, Prefect) provide checkpointing, dependency management, and scalability that ad hoc scripts cannot.
The LLM orchestrator's role is interpretation and adaptation, not computation — it reads stage outputs and decides whether to proceed, revise, or branch.
Data versioning (DVC) alongside code versioning is essential for full reproducibility of computational science.
Stage-level quality checks that block downstream execution are the primary mechanism for catching data quality issues early.
Containerisation (Docker) isolates pipeline stages and ensures portability across different computing environments.
